techStack.erp: SAP S/4HANA 2023 — single instance across all 3 entities since 2022 migration
techStack.s2p: SAP Ariba (indirect, €280M) + Jaggaer (strategic sourcing & SRM, €320M direct) — dual platform, partial integration
techStack.esourcing: Jaggaer eSourcing module — used for all events >€100k; Keelvar evaluated but not adopted
techStack.clm: Icertis CLM implemented 2023 — DocuSign integrated for eSignature, covers ~73% of active contracts
techStack.srm: Jaggaer SRM — supplier onboarding, qualification, scorecards, and performance management for strategic & preferred suppliers
techStack.ap: SAP FI native + Basware for e-invoicing network — 78% digital invoice receipt rate
techStack.analytics: Power BI (embedded dashboards) + Snowflake (data warehouse, T+1 refresh from SAP/Ariba/Jaggaer/Icertis)
techStack.other_tools: ServiceNow (IT procurement workflow), Concur (T&E), Coupa (evaluated 2023, not selected), EcoVadis (ESG ratings for top 50 suppliers)
techStack.ai_tools: Claude API (category intelligence + analytics chatbot pilot), GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI (RFx generator), XGBoost custom model (invoice anomaly detection, self-hosted), Microsoft Copilot (procurement chatbot pilot, 50 users)
techStack.integration: Ariba ↔ SAP: fully API-integrated. Jaggaer ↔ SAP: nightly sync (not real-time). Icertis ↔ SAP: contract-to-PO matching integrated. Snowflake: all sources connected via Fivetran, T+1 refresh.

general.org-structure: Category-led with 4 clusters: Direct, Indirect, Capex, IT
general.q-00-01: Automation maturity is mid-range. Core transactional flows (PO creation from approved requisitions, e-invoicing for top suppliers, and catalogue-based buying) are well automated. However, a significant portion of indirect spend — tail spend, project-based purchases, and non-PO invoices — still flows through email and manual processes. We are in the middle of a 2-year automation push targeting 85% touchless invoice processing and 75% catalogue coverage for indirect spend.
general.q-00-02: We have a formal AI roadmap for procurement, approved by the CFO in early 2025 with a 2-year horizon and €350k annual budget. The roadmap covers 8 use cases across 5 process steps. 4 use cases are currently live (spend classification, RFx generation, invoice anomaly detection, analytics chatbot pilot). The CPO is the executive sponsor; progress is reviewed quarterly by an AI steering group with IT and Finance representation.
general.q-00-03: Executive sponsorship is strong. The CPO has direct CFO backing for the AI roadmap and quarterly board-level reporting on AI ROI. The CTO is a co-sponsor for the data infrastructure workstream. Business unit heads are engaged at a category level — they see direct value from spend classification and supplier risk monitoring. Middle management engagement is more variable; some category managers are enthusiastic early adopters, others are more cautious about AI-assisted decision-making.
general.q-00-04: Top pain points the CPO wants AI to address: (1) Spend visibility — too much time spent manually extracting and cleaning spend data for management reporting; (2) RFx efficiency — sourcing cycle times are too long due to manual document preparation; (3) Supplier risk — reactive rather than proactive risk management, especially for tier-2 dependencies; (4) Contract compliance — significant off-contract spend leakage that is only discovered retroactively; (5) AP efficiency — too many manual touchpoints in invoice processing despite e-invoicing investment. Highest AI opportunity areas: invoice automation (already delivering strong ROI), should-cost modelling for direct spend negotiations, and AI contract intelligence.
general.q-00-05: Core sourcing and negotiation skills are strong — the team has been trained in structured methodology and applies it consistently. Category management capability is solid in our main clusters (Direct, IT, Facilities). Analytics capability is growing — 3 dedicated analysts and a Snowflake/Power BI infrastructure. Main gaps: AI literacy across the broader buying team (addressed through training programmes), advanced supplier risk capabilities (still largely manual), and should-cost modelling expertise for complex direct categories.
general.q-00-06: Spend data is centralised in Snowflake with feeds from SAP S/4HANA, Ariba, and Jaggaer — T+1 refresh. 85% of indirect spend is correctly classified via AI auto-classification. Contract data is ~73% digitised in Icertis. Supplier data is clean and deduplicated (MDM project completed 2023). The main data gap is non-PO spend (company cards, expense claims) which is not yet integrated into the central warehouse, representing ~€18M of blind spend annually.

s-org.q-a-01: CPO reports to CFO quarterly with a structured dashboard — solid cadence. However, board-level KPIs for procurement are not yet formalised as a standing agenda item. Scored 3: regular reporting and ExCo access are in place, but board accountability for procurement value delivery is still maturing.
s-org.q-a-02: A written mandate endorsed by the CFO exists and covers scope, ambition, and entities. Reviewed annually and communicated to BU heads. Scored 3: mandate is clear and documented, but not yet formally used as a resource allocation tool across all regions.
s-org.q-a-03: Front/back office separation completed 2 years ago — category managers own strategy, shared services own execution. 80% process standardisation across entities. Scored 3: structure is defined and largely applied, with some regional variation remaining in indirect categories.
s-org.q-a-04: Core capabilities in sourcing and negotiation are strong. Analytics team is growing (3 dedicated FTEs, Snowflake/Power BI). Gaps in AI literacy and advanced supplier risk. Scored 3: capabilities defined in a framework, gaps identified, structured development underway.
s-org.q-a-05: Only 5 of 42 FTEs have meaningful AI skills. Broader team has basic GenAI literacy but lacks hands-on tool experience. Scored 2: AI capability is fragmented — some individuals have skills but no structured AI capability across the function.
s-org.q-a-06: Change management is project-by-project, not a standing competency. The AI spend classification rollout had a full change programme (training, comms, hypercare) achieving 85% adoption. Scored 3: structured approach applied to major initiatives, not yet a consistent function-wide capability.
s-org.q-a-07: Procurement Academy with quarterly modules including AI/analytics content added in 2025. Training linked to performance objectives for senior buyers. AI skills framework under construction. Scored 3: role-based programmes exist, AI included, but framework is not yet fully formalised.

s-01.q-01-01: 2-year AI roadmap approved by CFO with €350k annual budget and CPO as executive sponsor. 4 of 8 planned use cases live. Progress tracked quarterly by cross-functional steering group. Scored 3: formal roadmap with budget and sponsor is in place — falls short of 4 because multi-year budget commitment and board-level tracking are not yet in place.
s-01.q-01-02: AI deployments require CPO and IT sign-off. DPIAs completed per tool. Basic audit trails in S2P platform but no centralised AI audit log. Scored 3: formal governance framework covers authorisation and data privacy, but audit trail consolidation is a known gap.
s-01.q-01-03: ROI tracked per live use case against pre-deployment baseline. Finance-validated savings reported quarterly to CFO. AI ROI included in broader savings pipeline reporting. Scored 3: formal tracking and finance validation for major deployments — not yet a portfolio-level investment management approach.
s-01.q-01-04: AI pipeline managed in a shared tracker reviewed monthly. Use cases prioritised by ROI and feasibility. 2 deprioritised but none formally retired. Scored 3: formal pipeline with owners and prioritisation criteria — lacks formal retirement process and portfolio investment framework.

s-02.q-02-01: All 8 strategic categories have formal strategies, refreshed annually with CPO sign-off. Sub-category plans exist for top 3 spend categories. Market and business changes incorporated in Q1 review. Scored 3: governance cadence and sign-off are established — falls short of 4 because not all categories are covered and the process is not yet fully auditable.
s-02.q-02-02: 85% of indirect spend auto-classified via AI (Snowflake + Claude). Taxonomy governed and reviewed quarterly. Supplier master deduplicated in 2023 MDM project. Scored 4: near-real-time AI auto-classification above 80% with maintained taxonomy — a genuine best-practice capability.
s-02.q-02-03: AI spend classification live and performing well. Claude API used for category intelligence briefings. Category managers can generate supplier landscape summaries and negotiation angles in natural language. Scored 3: AI formally deployed for multiple tasks but not yet automated across all category management activities.
s-02.q-02-04: Pilot for AI-assisted category strategy generation running with 2 category managers (IT and MRO). 65% reduction in drafting time reported. Full rollout planned Q3 2026. Scored 2: active pilot underway but not yet scaled — only 2 of 8 category managers trialling.
s-02.q-02-05: AI classification: €420k/yr finance-validated. Category intelligence briefings: ~60 hours/yr saved. Strategy AI pilot: 65% drafting time reduction (not yet formally measured). Scored 3: quantified outcomes tracked for live use cases — not yet portfolio-wide reporting with consistent methodology.

s-03.q-03-01: Fully digitised in Jaggaer SRM. Covers registration, qualification, compliance, data setup, system activation. Target 10-day cycle time met 78% of the time. Scored 3: standardised and digitally enabled process with cycle time targets — falls short of 4 due to supplier responsiveness delays and manual IT activation step.
s-03.q-03-02: 4-tier model (Strategic/Preferred/Approved/Transactional) built on spend, risk, and strategic relevance. Refreshed annually. Management approach differentiated by tier. Scored 3: formal model in place and systematically applied — lacks real-time, data-driven dynamism of a level 4.
s-03.q-03-03: D&B financial feeds for Strategic/Preferred. EcoVadis ESG for top 50. Geopolitical and supply chain risk monitored manually — news tool requires manual triage. No automated tier-2 visibility. Scored 3: risk monitoring across key types for strategic suppliers with mitigation links — incomplete coverage and no tier-2 automation.
s-03.q-03-04: Supplier master clean and deduplicated post-2023 MDM. D&B integrated for ~600 suppliers. EcoVadis loaded quarterly. Monthly refresh cycle — not real-time. Scored 3: clean and deduplicated with at least one external risk feed — real-time integration is the gap to level 4.
s-03.q-03-05: AI news monitoring pilot for 150 strategic suppliers using Azure Cognitive Services. 2 disruptions caught early, €1.2M risk mitigated. Weekly triage. Scored 3: AI-powered monitoring with automated alerts and defined response — not yet covering full supplier base or multiple risk dimensions autonomously.
s-03.q-03-06: No AI for supplier innovation scouting or autonomous qualification. 2 vendors evaluated (Scoutbee, Trademo), business case in progress. Scored 2: exploring AI tools, vendor evaluation underway, but no active pilot yet.
s-03.q-03-07: Jaggaer portal with automated quarterly scorecards for Strategic suppliers. Rule-based, not AI-enhanced. AI narrative features under evaluation for 2026. Scored 2: basic supplier portal in place — AI enhancement not yet deployed.

s-04.q-04-01: Scotwork-based negotiation methodology applied to all events >€500k. Pre-negotiation template (BATNA, reserve price, concession plan) mandatory. Post-negotiation reviews feed playbook. Scored 3: structured approach defined and consistently applied — AI coaching tools not yet in use.
s-04.q-04-02: Jaggaer stores all events, bids, and awards from 2021. ~3,000 structured historical events accessible for AI. Pre-2021 data in email archives. Scored 3: clean structured database accessible for AI — historical coverage gap pre-2021 prevents a level 4.
s-04.q-04-03: GPT-4o RFx generator live for all 8 strategic buyers. 40% cycle time reduction, from 3 days to <1 day. Bid analysis still manual. Award recommendations human-driven. Scored 4: AI formally embedded in the core sourcing workflow with measurable adoption and impact.
s-04.q-04-04: Tail spend semi-autonomous sourcing live for 12 categories (<€50k). 35% faster cycle time, 8-12% savings. Full autonomous agent pilots not yet started. Scored 2: active semi-autonomous pilot in defined scope — not yet piloting autonomous agents for the full RFx lifecycle.
s-04.q-04-05: RFx generator: 480 hours/yr saved across 8 buyers. Tail spend automation: 35% cycle time reduction, 8-12% savings. Total AI-driven sourcing value ~€1.8M in 2025. Scored 3: quantified outcomes tracked for AI-assisted events — not yet systematically reported as a sourcing AI portfolio.

s-05.q-05-01: 12 contract types covered by governed templates (supply agreements, NDA, services, T&M, frameworks). SharePoint-hosted, version-controlled. Non-standard deviations require legal sign-off. Reviewed 2024. Scored 4: comprehensive, governed library covering all major types with formal deviation approval — a mature capability.
s-05.q-05-02: 73% digitised in Icertis with structured metadata (parties, value, dates, terms). Integrated with SAP for PO matching. 27% legacy PDF digitisation in progress, target Q2 2026. Scored 3: majority digitised with structured metadata and system integration — falls short of 4 due to remaining legacy contracts.
s-05.q-05-03: Icertis AI for obligation extraction and renewal alerting live. Obligations tracked in dashboard. Alerts at 90/60/30 days. AI-assisted drafting available but low adoption. Scored 3: AI deployed for obligation tracking and renewal — full lifecycle AI management not yet achieved.
s-05.q-05-04: AI benchmarking pilot using Claude comparing payment terms, liability caps, price escalation vs. market norms. 8 contracts flagged with above-market payment terms, 3 with unusual liability. Validation in progress. Scored 2: active pilot for portfolio-wide AI analysis — not yet in production decision-making.
s-05.q-05-05: Icertis alerts on off-contract spend in categories with active framework agreements. €2.3M off-contract spend flagged last year and redirected. Real-time coverage limited to Icertis-integrated categories. Scored 3: AI compliance monitoring for key categories — not yet real-time across all spend.

s-06.q-06-01: Fully digital in SAP Ariba. Category, cost centre, preferred supplier mandatory at submission. 94% correctly categorised at point of request for indirect. Direct spend in S/4HANA — more manual. Scored 4: fully structured, integrated with real-time AI guidance for indirect — direct spend process is the remaining gap.
s-06.q-06-02: E-catalogues live for 15 categories, ~€120M (42% of indirect). 67% adoption for in-scope categories. Quarterly catalogue expansion programme. Target 75% coverage by end-2026. Scored 3: catalogues available for key categories, actively promoted — coverage and adoption gaps prevent a level 4.
s-06.q-06-03: Ariba Guided Buying live with preferred supplier recommendations and AI-based category routing. 78% preferred supplier compliance. AI approval routing for exceptions based on risk and value. Scored 3: AI deployed for guided buying and policy compliance — personalised AI recommendations not yet in place.
s-06.q-06-04: Microsoft Copilot procurement chatbot piloted with 50 IT department users in Q4 2025. High satisfaction, 35% regular engagement. Full rollout decision pending. Scored 2: active pilot for conversational AI in defined scope — not yet widely adopted.

s-07.q-07-01: POs digital in S/4HANA, electronically transmitted to 85% of suppliers. Digital acknowledgements from 70%. Real-time delivery tracking for direct. Indirect status relies on supplier self-reporting. Scored 4: fully digital, integrated with suppliers and real-time tracking — supplier acknowledgement gap noted.
s-07.q-07-02: SAP AI exception detection live: late delivery predictions, 3-way match discrepancies, duplicates. 200 exceptions/month, 60% auto-resolved. No dedicated AI delivery risk model beyond SAP standard. Scored 3: AI-driven exception detection in place — autonomous resolution and predictive risk not yet at level 4.
s-07.q-07-03: No autonomous PO agents in pilot. 35% of PO volume auto-created from catalogue requisitions (SAP built-in). Non-catalogue and strategic POs require buyer review. Monitoring market. Scored 2: basic automation in place for catalogue spend — autonomous agents not yet explored.

s-08.q-08-01: 78% digital invoice receipt (EDI + Ariba Network). Paper invoices 8%, mainly small local suppliers. Current touchless rate 71%, target 85% by end-2026. Scored 4: above 70% digital receipt with active programme to reach 80%+ — tracking toward best practice.
s-08.q-08-02: Self-hosted XGBoost model runs on 100% of PO invoices (€280M). 60% reduction in manual interventions. 88% automated 3-way match. Exception review only for flagged and non-PO invoices. Scored 4: AI handles the full PO invoice workflow with >85% touchless rate — high maturity.
s-08.q-08-03: 3-workstream roadmap to 85% touchless: supplier enablement (200 mid-tier suppliers), non-PO invoice AI extension, dynamic discounting pilot. IT delivery is the main constraint. Scored 3: active programme with defined target and timeline — not yet achieved for non-PO invoice types.
s-08.q-08-04: Cost per invoice: €4.20 → €2.80 (−33%). Cycle time: 8.2 → 4.1 days (−50%). Early payment discount capture: 12% → 34%. Duplicate payment incidents: 18 → 3/yr. FTE saving: 2.5 FTEs reallocated. Scored 3: quantified improvements across multiple AP metrics — not yet fully reported as a consistent AI portfolio metric.

s-09.q-09-01: Snowflake warehouse fed by S/4HANA, Ariba, Jaggaer, Icertis with T+1 refresh. Power BI accessible to all procurement without IT support. Finance and business units have read access. Scored 3: integrated near-real-time layer accessible organisation-wide — full real-time integration for all sources is the gap.
s-09.q-09-02: Claude claude-3-haiku analytics chatbot in pilot (CPO + 4 category managers, natural language queries). Automated anomaly detection on spend data weekly. Predictive spend forecasting in development. Scored 3: at least one AI capability in production (chatbot, anomaly detection) — predictive forecasting not yet live.
s-09.q-09-03: Category managers and analysts self-serve on Power BI. Chatbot extends NL querying to pilot group. IT still required for new dashboard development or source integration. Scored 3: standard and custom analysis without IT for most needs — full self-service for all analytical tasks not yet achieved.
s-09.q-09-04: Commodity price prediction model live for 5 direct categories: ±8% accuracy at 3-month horizon, feeds quarterly category reviews. Demand forecasting for indirect under construction. Scored 3: predictive analytics in production for at least one area with tracked accuracy — multi-area coverage still in development.

s-ai.q-ai-01: AI governance within IT framework. CPO + CTO sign-off required for new tools. DPIAs completed. Per-tool audit trails but no centralised AI audit log — a known gap. Scored 2: basic guidelines and some controls exist, but formal framework is not yet fully enforced or consolidated.
s-ai.q-ai-02: 3 AI upskilling programmes in 18 months: GenAI literacy (89% completion), hands-on AI tools workshop (12 FTEs), advanced analytics programme (5 FTEs). Role-based framework under construction for 2026. Scored 3: structured role-based programmes in place with defined capability levels — framework not yet fully formalised.
s-ai.q-ai-03: Spend classification and invoice AI: 100% embedded. RFx generator: 8/8 buyers active. Analytics chatbot: 4/5 users active. Category strategy AI: 2/8 piloting. Main barriers: workflow integration gaps and trust in AI outputs. Scored 3: most deployed tools actively used — inconsistency in pilot adoption and change programme maturity.
s-ai.q-ai-04: Vendor DPAs with Anthropic and Microsoft. Supplier data anonymised. Pricing data stays on-premise. Annual GDPR review. No formal AI ethics policy for procurement. Scored 2: aware of privacy requirements and some controls in place — formal AI ethics framework specific to procurement does not yet exist.

q-a-01.text: Procurement reports to the CFO on a quarterly basis. The CPO presents a procurement performance dashboard at each ExCo meeting. However, board-level accountability for specific procurement KPIs is not yet formalised — we have budget ownership and cost targets but no board-level scorecard specifically for procurement value delivery.

q-a-02.text: We have a written procurement mandate endorsed by the CFO that defines our scope across direct, indirect, capex and IT spend. It sets a clear ambition to capture 4–6% cost savings annually and to build a digitally-enabled, AI-ready function. The mandate is reviewed annually and communicated to all business unit heads.

q-a-03.text: We have separated strategic category management (front office) from operational buying and AP (back office) over the past 2 years. Category managers own strategy and supplier relationships; a shared services team handles PO processing and invoice management. Process standardisation across our 3 main entities is ~80% complete — some regional variation remains in indirect categories.

q-a-04.text: Core capabilities in sourcing, negotiation and supplier management are strong. Analytics capability is growing — we have 3 analysts dedicated to spend analytics. Main gaps remain in AI literacy across the broader team and in advanced supplier risk capabilities. We run annual skills assessments against a defined capability framework.

q-a-05.text: 5 of our 42 procurement FTEs have meaningful AI skills — primarily the analytics team and 2 category managers who have driven our AI use case pilots. The broader team has completed basic GenAI literacy training but lacks hands-on AI tooling experience. We have identified AI upskilling as a top priority for 2026.

q-a-06.text: Change management is handled on a project-by-project basis. Our AI spend classification rollout had a dedicated change programme — training, comms, hypercare — which drove 85% adoption in 6 months. We don't yet have a standing change management function within procurement; this is resourced per initiative.

q-a-07.text: We run a Procurement Academy with quarterly training modules covering negotiation, sourcing excellence, and digital tools. AI and analytics content was added in 2025. Training is linked to performance objectives for senior buyers and category managers. We are building a role-based AI skills framework to formalise AI as a career competency.

q-01-01.text: We have a 2-year AI roadmap for procurement approved by the CFO with a dedicated annual budget of €350k. The CPO is the executive sponsor. Progress is tracked via a quarterly AI steering group with representation from IT, Finance and Procurement. The roadmap covers 8 use cases across 5 process steps, of which 4 are live.

q-01-02.text: AI governance sits within our broader IT and data governance framework. Procurement AI deployments require CPO and IT sign-off. We have basic data privacy controls for each live AI tool — data processing agreements with vendors, no personal data in procurement AI systems. Audit trails are maintained in our S2P platform but not yet in a centralised AI audit log.

q-01-03.text: We track ROI for each live AI use case against a pre-deployment baseline. The spend classification tool saves ~€420k/yr in reclassification effort (finance-validated). The RFx generator reduces cycle time by 40% (tracked in Jaggaer). The invoice anomaly model is saving ~€180k/yr in FTE cost. We report AI ROI quarterly to the CFO alongside the broader savings pipeline.

q-01-04.text: We manage an AI use case pipeline in a shared tracking document reviewed monthly by the AI steering group. Currently 4 use cases live, 2 in pilot (supplier risk radar, analytics chatbot), 3 in backlog. Use cases are prioritised by ROI potential and implementation feasibility. We have not yet formally retired any use case but have deprioritised 2 that proved technically infeasible.

q-02-01.text: All 8 strategic categories have formal category strategies documented and refreshed annually. Category plans are signed off by the CPO and category sponsor in the business. We hold a category strategy review in Q1 each year where market developments and business priorities are incorporated. Sub-category plans exist for the top 3 spend categories.

q-02-02.text: 85% of our indirect spend is correctly classified using our AI spend classification tool (live since Q3 2024). Direct spend is handled differently — classified at PO level in SAP. Taxonomy is governed by our analytics team and reviewed quarterly. Supplier deduplication was completed as part of the MDM project in 2023 — we now maintain a clean supplier master with ~1,200 active suppliers.

q-02-03.text: Our AI spend classification is the most mature AI use case in category management — it runs continuously on our Snowflake warehouse and auto-classifies 85% of indirect spend without human review. We also use Claude API for category intelligence briefings — category managers can query market data, generate supplier landscape summaries, and get negotiation angle suggestions in natural language.

q-02-04.text: We are in early pilot for AI-assisted category strategy generation. Claude generates a first-draft category strategy based on spend data, market context, and historical sourcing event data. 2 category managers are trialling this for IT and MRO categories. Initial feedback is positive — strategies that took 3 days to write now take half a day. Full rollout planned for Q3 2026.

q-02-05.text: AI spend classification: €420k/yr savings in reclassification effort, validated by Finance. Category intelligence briefings (Claude): estimated 1.5 hours saved per category strategy refresh per category manager — approximately 60 hours/yr across the team. Category strategy AI pilot: not yet formally measured, but user feedback shows 65% reduction in strategy drafting time.

q-03-01.text: Supplier onboarding is fully digitised in Jaggaer SRM. The process covers registration, qualification questionnaire, compliance screening (sanctions, ESG basics), and SAP master data creation. Target cycle time is 10 business days for standard suppliers. We hit this 78% of the time — main delays are supplier responsiveness and manual IT system activation.

q-03-02.text: We use a 4-tier segmentation: Strategic (15 suppliers), Preferred (85 suppliers), Approved (400 suppliers), Transactional (700+). Strategic suppliers receive quarterly business reviews, joint KPI tracking, and executive engagement. Preferred suppliers are reviewed semi-annually. The segmentation model was rebuilt in 2024 based on spend, risk score, and strategic relevance — it is refreshed annually.

q-03-03.text: We monitor financial risk for Strategic and Preferred suppliers using Dun & Bradstreet feeds (credit risk, financial health scores). ESG monitoring is in place for 50 strategic suppliers via EcoVadis ratings. Geopolitical and supply chain risk is monitored manually — we use a news monitoring tool but it requires manual triage. We have no automated tier-2 visibility for any category.

q-03-04.text: Supplier master data is clean and deduplicated following our 2023 MDM project. D&B financial data is integrated for ~600 suppliers. EcoVadis ESG scores are loaded quarterly for top suppliers. We do not yet have real-time integration — data feeds run on a monthly refresh cycle. The SAP master is the golden source; Jaggaer syncs nightly.

q-03-05.text: Our supplier risk radar (AI news monitoring) is in pilot for 150 strategic suppliers. The tool uses Azure Cognitive Services to scan news feeds and flag risk signals — financial distress, litigation, geopolitical events, ESG controversies. Alerts are triaged by the SRM team 3x per week. 2 disruptions were caught early in the pilot (one sub-tier financial issue, one sanctions risk) — estimated €1.2M risk avoided.

q-03-06.text: We are not yet using AI for supplier innovation scouting or autonomous qualification. This is in our AI backlog — we have evaluated 2 vendors (Scoutbee, Trademo) but have not yet committed to a pilot. The business case is being built for our direct categories where new supplier scouting is most valuable.

q-03-07.text: We use our Jaggaer supplier portal for performance tracking — quarterly scorecards covering delivery, quality, and commercial KPIs are shared automatically with Strategic suppliers. The portal is not AI-enhanced — it's rule-based. We are evaluating adding AI-generated performance narratives and early warning flags to the portal in 2026.

q-04-01.text: We have a structured negotiation methodology (based on Scotwork) applied across all significant sourcing events (>€500k). Buyers complete a preparation template covering BATNA, reserve price, concession plan, and key tactics before every negotiation. Post-negotiation reviews are mandatory for strategic categories — learnings are captured in our negotiation playbook.

q-04-02.text: Historical RFx data is stored in Jaggaer — all events, bids, award decisions, and bid breakdowns from 2021 onwards are structured and searchable. We have ~3,000 historical sourcing events in the system. This data is now being used to train our AI RFx template generator and as context for should-cost modelling. Pre-2021 data is in email archives — not structured for AI use.

q-04-03.text: Our GPT-4o RFx template generator is fully live for our strategic sourcing team. It generates first-draft RFx documents from a category brief, historical event data, and market context. 8 buyers use it for every significant event — time from scope to RFx launch reduced from 3 days to under 1 day. Award recommendations are still human-driven. Bid analysis automation is in the pipeline.

q-04-04.text: We are not yet running autonomous sourcing agents. We are piloting semi-autonomous sourcing for tail spend categories (spend <€50k) using Jaggaer's built-in automation — buyers approve the scope and the system runs a simplified RFx and auto-awards to the lowest compliant bid. 12 categories in scope. Results so far: 35% faster cycle time, consistent savings of 8–12%.

q-04-05.text: RFx AI template generator: 40% reduction in RFx drafting time across 8 buyers — estimated 480 hours/yr saved. Tail spend semi-autonomous sourcing: 35% cycle time reduction, 8–12% average savings vs. prior contract. Total AI-driven sourcing value estimated at €1.8M in 2025, including 2 large negotiations where AI prep directly influenced outcomes.

q-05-01.text: We have a standard contract template library managed jointly by procurement and legal. It covers 12 major contract types (supply agreements, NDA, services, T&M, framework agreements). Templates are stored in SharePoint and version-controlled. Non-standard deviations require legal sign-off. The library was last comprehensively reviewed in 2024 and is updated when new regulatory requirements emerge.

q-05-02.text: ~73% of active contracts are digitised in Icertis (our CLM system, implemented in 2023). Key metadata (parties, value, start/end date, notice periods, key commercial terms) is captured as structured fields. Remaining 27% are legacy contracts in PDF — a digitisation project is underway targeting completion by Q2 2026. Icertis is integrated with SAP for contract-to-PO matching.

q-05-03.text: We use Icertis AI features for obligation extraction and renewal alerting — these are built into our Icertis licence. Obligations for strategic contracts are automatically extracted and loaded into a tracking dashboard. Renewal alerts fire 90, 60, and 30 days before expiry. AI-assisted drafting is available but adoption is low — buyers revert to templates rather than using AI drafting.

q-05-04.text: We are piloting AI contract intelligence for commercial benchmarking — using Claude to compare payment terms, liability caps, and price escalation clauses across our portfolio vs. market norms. Early findings have identified 8 contracts with above-market payment terms and 3 with unusual liability exposure. This is currently being validated before we use it to drive renegotiations.

q-05-05.text: Icertis alerts us when purchasing happens from non-preferred suppliers in categories with active framework agreements. Last year this flagged €2.3M of off-contract spend in IT and Facilities — which was subsequently redirected. We don't yet have real-time AI compliance monitoring across all categories — coverage is limited to the categories where Icertis catalogue integration is in place.

q-06-01.text: Requisitioning is fully digital in SAP Ariba for indirect spend. All mandatory fields (category, cost centre, preferred supplier, business justification) are enforced at submission. Data quality for indirect spend is high — 94% of requisitions are correctly categorised at point of request. Direct spend requisitions flow through SAP S/4HANA production planning — a different, more manual process.

q-06-02.text: E-catalogues are live for our top 15 indirect categories covering ~€120M of addressable spend (about 42% of total indirect). Catalogue adoption sits at 67% for in-scope categories. Procurement reviews catalogue coverage quarterly and adds 2–3 new catalogues per year. We are pushing to reach 75% catalogue coverage by end of 2026.

q-06-03.text: We use Ariba Guided Buying for preferred supplier recommendations and category-based routing. The AI features in Ariba automatically suggest preferred suppliers based on category and prior purchasing history. We see ~78% compliance with preferred supplier suggestions in guided buying. AI-powered approval routing is live — exceptions are automatically routed based on risk and value thresholds.

q-06-04.text: We piloted a Microsoft Copilot-powered procurement chatbot in Q4 2025 — a virtual assistant that employees can use to check procurement policies, find preferred suppliers, and initiate a requisition via natural language. The pilot ran with 50 users in the IT department. Satisfaction scores were high but adoption was moderate — 35% of target users engaged regularly. Full rollout decision pending.

q-07-01.text: POs are generated in SAP S/4HANA and transmitted electronically to ~85% of our suppliers via EDI or Ariba Network. Supplier acknowledgements are received digitally for ~70% of POs. Real-time delivery status is tracked for direct material POs through SAP integrated with our WMS. Indirect PO status tracking relies on supplier self-reporting in the Ariba portal.

q-07-02.text: We use SAP's built-in AI features for PO exception detection — late delivery predictions, 3-way match discrepancies, and duplicate PO alerts. The system flags ~200 exceptions per month for human review; of these, ~60% are resolved automatically by routing rules. We do not yet have a dedicated AI model for delivery risk prediction beyond SAP's standard functionality.

q-07-03.text: We are not actively piloting autonomous PO agents. SAP's automated PO creation from approved requisitions (for catalogue items) handles ~35% of our PO volume without buyer intervention. For non-catalogue and strategic POs, buyer review is still required. We are monitoring agentic PO solutions in the market but have not committed to a pilot in 2026.

q-08-01.text: We receive 78% of invoices digitally — EDI for our top 50 suppliers, PDF e-invoice for mid-tier, and Ariba Network integration for the remainder. Paper invoices account for ~8% of volume, mostly from small local suppliers. Our current touchless processing rate is 71% — the target is 85% by end of 2026. The main barrier is supplier enablement for the remaining 22% who are not yet on our e-invoicing programme.

q-08-02.text: Our XGBoost anomaly detection model is the most mature AI deployment in the company. It runs on 100% of PO invoices (€280M annual throughput) and flags anomalies — duplicate invoices, pricing deviations from PO, quantity mismatches, suspicious payment patterns. The model reduces manual intervention by 60%. 3-way match is automated for 88% of PO invoices. Manual review is reserved for flagged exceptions and non-PO invoices.

q-08-03.text: Our roadmap to 85% touchless by end-2026 involves 3 workstreams: (1) supplier enablement — moving the remaining 200 medium suppliers to Ariba Network or structured e-invoice format; (2) non-PO invoice AI — extending our model to non-PO invoice types which are currently manual; (3) payment timing optimisation — piloting dynamic discounting for top 30 suppliers. IT delivery is the main constraint.

q-08-04.text: AI invoice processing improvements vs. 2023 baseline: cost per invoice reduced from €4.20 to €2.80 (−33%); average processing cycle time from 8.2 days to 4.1 days (−50%); early payment discount capture increased from 12% to 34% of eligible invoices; duplicate payment incidents reduced from 18 to 3 per year. Total FTE saving from AP automation: ~2.5 FTEs reallocated to value-added analysis work.

q-09-01.text: We have a centralised Snowflake data warehouse fed by SAP S/4HANA, Ariba, Jaggaer, and Icertis. Spend, supplier, contract, PO, and invoice data are integrated with a T+1 refresh for most sources (some near-real-time for SAP). Power BI dashboards are available to all procurement staff without IT support. Finance and business stakeholders have read access to the main spend dashboard.

q-09-02.text: Our procurement analytics chatbot (Claude claude-3-haiku) is in pilot — CPO and 4 category managers can query our Snowflake data in natural language and receive AI-generated summaries with charts. We also have automated anomaly detection running on our spend data — flagging unusual spend patterns, new suppliers, and category mix shifts weekly. Predictive spend forecasting is in development for 2026.

q-09-03.text: Category managers, the analytics team, and senior buyers can self-serve on Power BI without IT. The analytics chatbot extends this to natural language querying for the pilot group. IT is still required for new dashboard development or integration of new data sources. Our goal is to reach full self-service for all analytical needs for the 15 most senior procurement staff by end of 2026.

q-09-04.text: We have a commodity price prediction model for our top 5 direct material categories — built on historical price data and publicly available commodity indices. Accuracy at 3-month horizon is ±8%, which is within the range useful for hedging decisions. The model feeds into our quarterly category strategy reviews. We are building a demand forecasting model for indirect categories in 2026.

q-ai-01.text: AI governance for procurement sits within our IT AI governance framework. All new AI tools require a Data Privacy Impact Assessment (DPIA) and joint sign-off from the CPO and CTO. Vendor agreements include data processing clauses and model transparency requirements. We maintain a register of all live AI tools and their data flows. Audit trails are maintained per tool but not yet consolidated into a central AI audit log — this is a known gap we are addressing.

q-ai-02.text: We have run 3 AI upskilling programmes for procurement in the past 18 months: (1) GenAI literacy for all 42 FTEs (4-hour online programme, 89% completion); (2) AI tools hands-on workshop for buyers and category managers (12 FTEs, full day); (3) AI for analytics — advanced programme for the 5-person analytics team. We are building a role-based AI skills framework with defined capability levels per role for 2026.

q-ai-03.text: AI tool adoption is tracked per deployment. Spend classification: 100% automated — no adoption issue. RFx generator: 8 of 8 target buyers using it weekly. Invoice AI: fully embedded, no opt-out. Analytics chatbot: 4 of 5 target users active. Category strategy AI: 2 of 8 category managers piloting. Main barriers to adoption are workflow integration (tools not embedded natively in our S2P platform) and trust in AI outputs for complex decisions.

q-ai-04.text: We have data privacy agreements with all AI vendors (Anthropic, Microsoft Azure, our XGBoost model is self-hosted). Supplier data used in AI models is anonymised or covered by existing data processing agreements. Pricing data stays within our infrastructure — we do not send commercially sensitive bid data to third-party AI services. GDPR compliance is reviewed annually for all AI tools. We do not yet have a formal AI ethics policy specific to procurement.
